   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
   leaves to match: `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`,
   `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
   (`init.rs`, tests).

   Note: can a single field be defined as an type or enum instead
   of a struct and maybe eliminate the `args.<leaf>.<leaf>` name
   issue.
3. **Investigate `linkme` for subcommand registration.**

content.text:
Reviewed both changes.

## `## In Progress` intro (lines 12–14) — needs a grammar fix

The new text isn't grammatical:

> The overview from ## Todo list which should **an overview** of the problem and a list **things** to do.

- "which should an overview" — missing verb ("which should *be*").
- "a list things to do" — missing "of" ("a list *of* things").
- `## Todo` should be backticked — every other mention in this file is `` `## Todo` `` / `` `## In Progress` ``.

Suggested rewrite (keeps your intent — copied overview + to-do list, then the plan/ladder):

```
When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation
```

## Item #1

The mechanical nits are fixed — indent on line 40 is now 5 spaces, "the the" is gone. Good.

Two things on line 47, `This supercedes what's in CLAUDE.md`:

- **Typo: "supercedes" → "supersedes"** (the `s`-spelling is the standard one).
- It's now *too* vague. My earlier point was that the fragment was cryptic — the fix is to name the edit targets, not drop them. "what's in CLAUDE.md" makes the implementer go hunting. The previous version at least pointed at Versioning + checklist + the `## In Progress` format. A todo entry should stay brief, but a section pointer earns its keep here. And note this still drops the reference to the two chores subsections that also encode the old rule (`chores-10` "Process: delete a Todo item when it goes In Progress", `chores-11` "Keeping the active task in `## Todo`") — whoever implements #1 needs to reconcile those too.

Minor: bullet-final punctuation is inconsistent — line 45 ends with a period, lines 40/42/43 don't. Pick all-or-none.

Want me to apply the intro rewrite and the "supersedes" fix?

message.content: yes, apply the intro rewrite and the supersedes fix

content.content:
255	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
256	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
257	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
258	`:`). General markdown reference:
259	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
260	publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
261	de-facto reference implementation is
262	[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).
263	
264	### Versioning
265	
266	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
267	on scope:
268	
269	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
270	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
271	- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
272	  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
273	  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.
274	
275	The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
276	starting.
277	
278	A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
279	existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **deletes that
280	`## Todo` entry in the same commit** (the version-bump / plan
281	commit). `## In Progress` is the sole record of that work until
282	close-out, when it moves to `## Done`; a cycle that merges
283	several entangled `## Todo` items deletes all of them. A `## Todo`
284	entry that duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
285	bug.
286	
287	For multi-step:
288	
289	1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
290	   marker.
291	2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
292	   needed).
293	3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
294	   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
295	   marker.
296	
297	Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
298	`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
299	`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the
300	format. When starting a new step, the *first* edit is to mark
301	that step `(current)` in `notes/todo.md` — before any code/doc
302	work — so the In Progress view reflects what's actually
303	happening. The flip back to `(done)` is part of the pre-commit
304	checklist (item 6).
305	
306	**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
307	semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
308	component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
309	`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
310	below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
311	adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
312	doubles typing per commit.
313	
314	The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
315	amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
316	to see which commits were exploratory vs final.
317	
318	For decomposition finer than `X.Y.Z-N` — sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M`)
319	or sub-sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M-K`) — see
320	[Sub-step Workflow](#sub-step-workflow). The cycle close-out
321	choice (squash to one commit vs land each sub-step separately)
322	is made at close-out, not cycle start.
323	
324	See also [`notes/substep-protocol.md`](notes/substep-protocol.md)
325	for the full protocol — when to use, per-substep contract
326	(`cargo test --bins` is non-negotiable), navigation, the validated
327	close-out squash recipe, and recovery. Revset primitives the
328	protocol relies on (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) live in
329	[`notes/jj-revsets.md`](notes/jj-revsets.md).

publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
de-facto reference implementation is

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump

  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.

starting.

existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **deletes that

bug.

For multi-step:

1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
   marker.
2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
   needed).
3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates

Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the

that step `(current)` in `notes/todo.md` — before any code/doc
work — so the In Progress view reflects what's actually
happening. The flip back to `(done)` is part of the pre-commit

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
to see which commits were exploratory vs final.

For decomposition finer than `X.Y.Z-N` — sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M`)
or sub-sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M-K`) — see
[Sub-step Workflow](#sub-step-workflow). The cycle close-out
choice (squash to one commit vs land each sub-step separately)
is made at close-out, not cycle start.

See also [`notes/substep-protocol.md`](notes/substep-protocol.md)
for the full protocol — when to use, per-substep contract
(`cargo test --bins` is non-negotiable), navigation, the validated
close-out squash recipe, and recovery. Revset primitives the
protocol relies on (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) live in
[`notes/jj-revsets.md`](notes/jj-revsets.md).

- **Bullet punctuation** — all four bullets end with periods now (was just the last one).

One deliberate choice: you gave me lines 278–300, but I referenced the **section name + quoted rule text** rather than the line numbers. Line numbers in CLAUDE.md drift every time that file is edited, so a numeric range in a durable todo would go stale; the section heading and the quoted `**deletes…**` string stay findable. content.text:
Yes, that makes sense — and it's a nice bit of dogfooding. To restate it back so we're aligned:

For a **single-step** cycle, "pickup" and "close-out" both happen before the one commit. So I'll *populate* `## In Progress` with #1's text as the first edit, you review the working copy (the `## In Progress` block included) during the code loop, then I *clear* it and write the `## Done` one-liner at close-out. The single commit's diff shows `## In Progress` unchanged (`_No cycle currently in progress._` → same) — the populated state lived only in the working copy you reviewed. That's expected and fine; multi-step is where `## In Progress` actually persists into intermediate commits.

## Proposed plan — single-step, `docs:`, version **0.56.0**

**Cargo.toml** — `0.55.0` → `0.56.0`.

**notes/todo.md**
- Move #1's text out of `## Todo` into `## In Progress` (overview + to-do list + a one-line single-step "plan") — dogfooding the new rule.
- `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` to renumber the remaining entries.
- At close-out: clear `## In Progress` back to `_No cycle currently in progress._`, add a `## Done` one-liner + `[N]` ref.

**CLAUDE.md `### Versioning` sweep**
- Rewrite the delete-on-pickup paragraph (the `**deletes that ## Todo entry**` rule): pickup *moves* the entry's text into `## In Progress`; add a sentence noting single-step's `## In Progress` is a transient working-copy/review artifact not recorded in history.
- Pre-commit checklist item (b) — "delete that `## Todo` entry" → "move it into `## In Progress`".
- Glance item 7 ("move the entry from `## In Progress` to `## Done`") and the "Multi-step cycles surface the ladder…" sentence; tighten if needed.

**notes/chores/chores-11.md**
- Backfill the `## feat: validate-todo + fix-todo (0.55.0)` section's missing `Commits:` line (`[[16]]` → `95ef1987a65a`).
- Add a new `## docs: … (0.56.0)` section (intro + the design point about transient single-step `## In Progress`).

Then `cargo fmt`/`clippy`/`test`, `cargo install --locked`, and `vc-x1 push main`.

Provisional commit title: `docs: codify In-Progress pickup workflow (0.56.0)` (49 chars).

Shall I proceed, starting with the Cargo.toml bump?

message.content: That looks good, but here's a thought, we use 3 jj nodes to do the 3 "sub-steps" 1) Update chores + In Progress 2) do the work 3) close-out. With reviews at each sub-step and then after the "close-out" we squash so there is only a single commit. Thoughts?

content.text:
I like it — and it actually fits your earlier "In Progress info at review time" point *better* than the single-step version.

## Why it's better

With single-step, the populated `## In Progress` lived only in transient working-copy state. With 3 nodes:

- **Node 1** commit has `## In Progress` populated → it's a real (mutable, pre-squash) committed revision you review in your editor with full file context.
- **Node 2** still has it populated while the CLAUDE.md rewrite lands.
- **Node 3** clears it + adds the `## Done` line.
- The squash erases `## In Progress` churn from the final `0.56.0` commit on `main` — exactly your intent.

So the In-Progress content is reviewable as committed history *during* the cycle, then gone from permanent history. Cleaner story.

## It's already CLAUDE.md machinery

This is the **Sub-step Workflow's commit-first review model** (commit each node, you review the committed revision, I iterate via `jj squash --into @-`) plus the **squash close-out** option — both already documented. Nothing new to invent; we're just applying it to a 3-node cycle.

## Two things to settle

**Numbering** — I'd version the nodes `0.56.0-0`, `0.56.0-1`, `0.56.0-2` (Cargo.toml + commit titles per the sub-step rule), then squash to `0.56.0`. Node 3 / the squash is where Cargo.toml drops the suffix.

**The one real cost** — CLAUDE.md mandates the full cargo cycle (`fmt`/`clippy`/`test` + `cargo install --locked`) *per sub-step*. This cycle is docs-only — nothing compiles differently — so `cargo install` 3× is pure waste. I'd suggest: skip `cargo install` on nodes 1–2, run the full cycle once at close-out (node 3). `cargo fmt`/`test` are cheap enough to run per node if you want the habit. Your call.

Also: each node pairs an app commit with a `.claude` commit (ochid trailers both ways) — 3 pairs — and `.claude` keeps its per-node commits as the journal (only the app side squashes).

One honest caveat: for a ~3-paragraph docs change this is a fair bit of ceremony. It's worth it if you want to dogfood the sub-step workflow and review committed revisions; a single-step with per-file checkpoints would also do the job. You've clearly thought about it and I think it's a sound choice — just naming the tradeoff.

Confirm the `0.56.0-0/-1/-2` numbering and the cargo-cycle call, and I'll start with node 1 (chores opener + populate `## In Progress` + version bump).

message.content: You're correct for this trivial change this is to much ceremony, but as a dogfooding excercise I think it's worth it. In particular, should we create a description for each of these sub-steps, I hadn't considered that as I wasn't contemplating doing pushes to github, but adding a description doesn't mean pushing. But I'd would like to use zed to review the changes and that may mean we need a description, I'm not sure. Thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — each sub-step gets a description, and you've got the key fact right: **a description is local-only.** `jj describe` / `jj commit -m` writes commit metadata in your local repo; `jj git push` is the *only* thing that touches GitHub, and in this plan it happens exactly once, at close-out, on the squashed commit.

A few points:

**It's not really optional under the protocol.** The commit-first review model has you `jj commit` each sub-step (not `jj new` undescribed nodes), and the ochid trailers live *in the description*. So a description per node is already required by the sub-step workflow — describing them isn't extra ceremony you're adding, it's the workflow as written.

**Zed angle.** Zed doesn't need a description to *render* a diff — a diff is a diff. But it needs one for you to *pick the right commit*. Three nodes all showing `(no description set)` in the log / commit graph are indistinguishable; with titles you can tell node 1 from node 3 at a glance. Practical setup:

- Configure jj's diff tool once in `~/.config/jj/config.toml`: `diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]`.
- Review each node with `jj diff -r @-` (the node I just committed — `jj commit` leaves `@` empty on top) or `jj diff -r <change-id>` for an earlier one.
- Descriptions make `jj log` readable so you know which `-r` to pass.

**Efficiency suggestion.** Since the squash discards the per-node descriptions, don't triplicate the polished file-by-file body. Per node: a real title (`… (0.56.0-N)`) + a short body (1–3 bullets of what that node did) + the ochid trailer. The full file-by-file body gets composed *once*, for the squashed `0.56.0` commit, at the close-out `vc-x1 push main` message step. Per-node descriptions stay useful for navigation without you writing three throwaway essays.

So the flow is: per node — edit → `jj commit -m … -R .` + paired `.claude` commit → you review in Zed → iterate via `jj squash --into @-` if needed. Close-out — squash the 3 app nodes, `jj describe` the result with the final message, one `vc-x1 push main`.

Confirmed numbering `0.56.0-0/-1/-2` → squash `0.56.0`, full cargo cycle once at node 3. Ready to start node 1 (chores opener + populate `## In Progress` + version bump) on your go.

message.content: The "bot" repo is a linear history and we should "minimize/never" squash of it. Hence I'm thinking we shouldn't do the duplicate "title/body" ceremony as we do with a "multi-step", thoughts?

content.text:
Agree. The "same title/body in both repos" rule pays off only when both repos keep parallel commits — normal multi-step, no squash. Here the app side squashes 3 → 1 while `.claude` stays linear, so copying the three app messages onto `.claude` would permanently store three `.claude` commits echoing app messages that no longer exist on the app side. Pointless duplication.

It also lines up with a rule CLAUDE.md *already* has, that `vc-x1 push` quietly overrides: **"Session-repo body: bullets describe in-session activity rather than code changes."** Push copies the app message because it composes one message for both commits — but the per-sub-step `.claude` commits here are plain `jj commit`s, not push, so we can follow the session-activity rule as written.

**Concrete shape:**
- App nodes `0.56.0-0/-1/-2` — real title + 1–3 bullet body, but throwaway (squashed at close-out). The polished file-by-file body is composed once for the squashed `0.56.0`, at the close-out push.
- `.claude` nodes — their own lightweight session-journal messages (what the session did that node). No app-message copying. Kept, not squashed.

**The one consequence to handle — ochid.** ochid pairs commits by change ID, and the app squash collapses 3 app change IDs into 1. The clean fix needs no fixup: we squash the app stack *downward into node1* (the natural direction), so **node1's change ID survives as the final `0.56.0` commit**. Therefore — point every `.claude` node's `ochid` at **node1's app change ID** from the start. node1's ID exists the moment we commit node1, so all three `.claude` commits can reference it, and after the squash they're all already correct. The app `0.56.0` commit just gets its ochid (→ the final `.claude` commit) set fresh when we compose the close-out message — normal close-out work.

So: zero ochid fixup, at the cost of committing upfront to "squash into node1" — which is the obvious direction anyway, and we've already decided to squash.

This is good dogfooding signal: CLAUDE.md doesn't currently address sub-step-squash crossed with the linear bot repo. I'll capture it in this cycle's chores section, and it may deserve a follow-up todo to codify (the squash-into-node1 ochid convention, the `.claude` session-message exemption).

Ready to start node 1 on your go.

lastPrompt: The "bot" repo is a linear history and we should "minimize/never" squash of it. Hence I'm thinking we shouldn't do the duplicate "title/body" ceremony as we do with a "multi-step", thoughts?

message.content:
Hmm, I think there is a misunderstanding, on these sub-steps there will be only
one jj node on the "bot" repo and thus no description is needed until the close out.
On the "code" side all three nodes can point to the @ node on the "bot" side.
I definitely might be wrong, but that's what I think.

content.text:
You're right — and it's cleaner than what I described. I over-applied CLAUDE.md's per-sub-step `.claude` cadence. Let me concede that and check the mechanics, since the whole thing rests on one jj fact.

**The load-bearing fact: jj change IDs are stable across rewrites.** A change ID is assigned once and survives working-copy snapshots (editing files in `@`), `jj describe`, `jj squash`, and rebases. It's only replaced by `jj commit` (which creates a *new* empty `@` — but the finalized commit *keeps* the old `@`'s change ID) or `jj new`.

So your model holds:

- The `.claude` `@` has a change ID **right now**, stable as long as we don't `jj commit -R .claude` or `jj new -R .claude`. Session data accumulating into it = snapshots = ID unchanged. `jj describe` at close-out = ID unchanged. Push's finalize `jj commit` = the finalized `.claude` commit *keeps* that ID.
- Therefore all three app sub-step commits carry the **same** `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-@-id>`, and it stays valid forever — through close-out and beyond. No N:1 mess, no fixup. One `.claude` node, one description, written once at close-out.

**Bonus: both directions are deterministic from cycle start.** App node1 = the current app `@` `ynunuxxo`. We squash node2/node3 *downward into node1*, so the final `0.56.0` commit's change ID is also `ynunuxxo`. So `.claude`'s close-out ochid → `/ynunuxxo` is known now too. Zero fixup either direction.

**This does contradict CLAUDE.md's `.claude` cadence section** — which explicitly *rejected* "accumulate across the cycle, commit once at close-out." But the rejection's rationale was "preserves flexibility — any sub-step can be promoted to its own push." A **squash** close-out voids that: no sub-step gets promoted. So the honest rule is:

- **keep-separate** close-out → per-sub-step `.claude` commits (current rule).
- **squash** close-out → one `.claude` node, committed once. Your model.

The only thing given up is sub-step-granular session snapshots — irrelevant for a 3-node docs cycle.

So, practically: capture the `.claude` `@` change ID once at node-1 start (`vc-x1 chid -s bot -L`); all three app ochids use it; `.claude` gets its single description at close-out. And this is solid dogfooding signal — I'll note the cadence carve-out in the chores section and it likely deserves a follow-up todo to codify in CLAUDE.md.

Ready to start node 1 on your go.

1	# Todo
2	
3	This file contains near term tasks with a short description
4	and reference links to more details.
5	
6	Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
7	line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
8	pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.
9	
10	## In Progress
11	
12	When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
13	problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
14	by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
15	   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
16	   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
17	   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
18	   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation
19	
20	**Codify the move-into-`## In Progress` workflow.**
21	
22	- On pickup, move the `## Todo` item's text into
23	  `## In Progress` so the section is self-describing (not
24	  a bare ladder).
25	- Also capture it in the chores opener so the history
26	  is readable without `git` spelunking.
27	- At close-out completely remove the item from "In Progress".
28	- Add a one liner to `## Done` and a reference to the
29	  chores entry.
30	
31	Implementation: CLAUDE.md's `### Versioning` section
32	currently says a pickup "**deletes that `## Todo` entry
33	in the same commit**" — change that rule to *move* the
34	entry's text into `## In Progress` instead, and sweep the
35	section's other `## In Progress` / `## Todo` mentions to
36	match the new shape.
37	
38	Plan — 3 sub-steps squashed into one `0.56.0` commit:
39	   - 0.56.0-0 chores opener + populate `## In Progress` +
40	     version bump (current)
41	   - 0.56.0-1 rewrite CLAUDE.md `### Versioning` + checklist
42	     sweep
43	   - 0.56.0-2 close-out: clear `## In Progress`, add
44	     `## Done` entry
45	   - 0.56.0 squash sub-steps → single commit, push
46	
47	## Todo
48	
49	 A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
50	 highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
51	 Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
52	 in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.
53	
54	 Entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` … numbers in the source so
55	 you can grep, count, and reference them ("let's work on
56	 #3"). You don't hand-maintain the numbers — insert, delete,
57	 or reorder entries freely, then `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run`
58	 renumbers the list and normalizes continuation-line indent.
59	 `vc-x1 fix-todo` alone only previews; `vc-x1 validate-todo`
60	 is the read-only check.
61	
62	1. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
63	   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
64	   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
65	   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
66	   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
67	   leaves to match: `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`,
68	   `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
69	   (`init.rs`, tests).
70	
71	   Note: can a single field be defined as an type or enum instead
72	   of a struct and maybe eliminate the `args.<leaf>.<leaf>` name
73	   issue.
74	2. **Investigate `linkme` for subcommand registration.**
75	   Distributed-slice registry — each subcommand registers itself
76	   at link time; `main.rs` discovers them via the slice rather
77	   than matching a `Commands` enum. Reduces per-subcommand
78	   touchpoints from 3 (mod decl + enum variant + match arm) to 1
79	   (registration). Costs: loses compile-time exhaustiveness
80	   (missing registration = runtime gap); help-output ordering
81	   depends on link order unless sorted; macro-magic dependency.
82	   Revisit once the `0.50.0` trait sweep's per-arm cost has been
83	   felt under real "add a subcommand" load.
84	   <https://github.com/dtolnay/linkme>
85	3. **Investigate `inventory` as `linkme` alternative.** Same
86	   shape as `linkme` — runtime-iterable registry populated by
87	   `inventory::submit!` per subcommand. Trade-offs mirror
88	   linkme's. Pick one if/when the trait sweep's match becomes
89	   the felt bottleneck.
90	   <https://github.com/dtolnay/inventory>
91	4. **`por → dual` conversion.** Attach a `.claude`
92	   companion repo + `.vc-config.toml` to an existing por
93	   workspace; emit cross-links going forward. Manual
94	   setup on an external por workspace (2026-05-14)

98	   `vc-x1 finalize --repo .bot` on `../dicom-rs`
99	   (2026-05-14) honored `--repo` throughout actual
100	   operations, but `bm-track` still emitted

102	   `.claude` baked in. Cosmetic today; load-bearing once
103	   bot-repo name becomes configurable (see Symmetric
104	   `.vc-config.toml` schema entry below).
105	6. **por/dual parity + `dual → por` conversion.** Make
106	   `por` and `dual` first-class equals (dual is primary
107	   today, por bolted on); add `dual → por` conversion
108	   (detach the `.claude` companion). Builds on the
109	   `--scope` rollout below. Pre-design; goal + open

111	7. **forks-multi-user + bot-data-formats follow-through.**
112	   Design captured across two notes; concrete work to

115	   immutability enforcement; URL-shaped ochid (per-user

125	   up from cwd via existing `find_workspace_root`, then maps

## docs: codify In-Progress pickup workflow (0.56.0)

Codifies the move-into-`## In Progress` workflow — the
refined idea the `0.55.0` close-out queued as a `## Todo`
item. On pickup a `## Todo` item's text now *moves* into
`## In Progress` (the section becomes self-describing)
rather than being deleted; at close-out it is removed and a
`## Done` one-liner with a chores ref is added. CLAUDE.md's
`### Versioning` section carried the old delete-on-pickup
rule; this cycle rewrites it and sweeps the pre-commit
checklist to match.

Run as a 3-sub-step squash cycle (`0.56.0-0/-1/-2` →
`0.56.0`) that dogfoods the new rule on itself.

# References

through untouched. Flagging them as malformed input is a
separate follow-up captured in `## Todo`.

## feat: validate-todo + fix-todo (0.55.0)

Cycle close-out. The `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` subcommand
pair — a Rust replacement for `notes/fix-todo.py` — is in:
`validate-todo` checks `## Todo` / `## Bugs` numbering and
continuation indent, `fix-todo` renumbers and re-indents
(dry-run by default). Both share the `todo_helpers` core and
print an identical per-entry report line.

### As-built ladder

- 0.55.0-0 plan + version bump + chores opener
- 0.55.0-1 add validate-todo subcommand + todo_helpers
- 0.55.0-2 add fix-todo subcommand; docs sweep; delete
  notes/fix-todo.py
- 0.55.0 close-out

### Outcome

Two subcommands and a docs sweep over three steps, no
evaluation-gate detours — the subcommand work landed as
planned.

The one reversal was the opener's workflow experiment.
`0.55.0-0`'s `### Keeping the active task in ## Todo`
switched CLAUDE.md from delete-on-pickup to a keep-#1 model
(the picked-up `## Todo` entry stays under a `### Current In
Progress` heading, linked from `## In Progress`). It did its
job — the active task stayed visible in `## Todo` for easy
dogfooding mid-cycle — but at close-out the user judged it
not worth keeping. This commit reverts CLAUDE.md to plain
delete-on-pickup. A refined idea — move the picked-up entry
*into* `## In Progress` (self-describing, no heading hack)
and capture it in the chores opener — is queued as a
`## Todo` item for a later cycle.

Dropping `## Todo` item 1 and renumbering was done with
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` — the cycle's own tool on its
own backlog, and the close-out dogfood.

## docs: codify In-Progress pickup workflow (0.56.0)

Codifies the move-into-`## In Progress` workflow — the
refined idea the `0.55.0` close-out queued as a `## Todo`
item. On pickup a `## Todo` item's text now *moves* into
`## In Progress` (the section becomes self-describing)
rather than being deleted; at close-out it is removed and a
`## Done` one-liner with a chores ref is added. CLAUDE.md's
`### Versioning` section carried the old delete-on-pickup
rule; this cycle rewrites it and sweeps the pre-commit
checklist to match.

Run as a 3-sub-step squash cycle (`0.56.0-0/-1/-2` →
`0.56.0`) that dogfoods the new rule on itself.

Bullet 2 says "capture it in the chores opener," and I wrote a *paraphrase* summary instead of *capturing* the item. Progress` workflow the `0.55.0` close-out queued. Mirrored

- On pickup, move the `## Todo` item's text into
  `## In Progress` so the section is self-describing (not
  a bare ladder).
- Also capture it in the chores opener so the history

in the same commit**" — change that rule to *move* the
entry's text into `## In Progress` instead, and sweep the
section's other `## In Progress` / `## Todo` mentions to
match the new shape.

Plan — 3 sub-steps squashed into one `0.56.0` commit:

- 0.56.0-0 chores opener + populate `## In Progress` +

- 0.56.0-1 rewrite CLAUDE.md `### Versioning` + checklist
  sweep
- 0.56.0-2 close-out: clear `## In Progress`, add `## Done`
  entry

content.content:
430	commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
431	finds it.
432	
433	### Pre-commit checklist
434	
435	At cycle *start* (before the version bump): (a) backfill the
436	previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
437	commit's URL + full SHA — see
438	[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references); (b) if
439	this cycle picks up a `## Todo` item (or re-scopes `## In
440	Progress` to absorb one), delete that `## Todo` entry — see
441	[Versioning](#versioning).
442	
443	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
444	
445	1. `cargo fmt`
446	2. `cargo clippy`
447	3. `cargo test`
448	4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
449	   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
450	   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
451	   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
452	5. Retest after install
453	6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
454	   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
455	   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
456	   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
457	   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
458	   Versioning multi-step section.)
459	7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
460	   move the entry from `## In Progress` to `## Done`.
461	8. Update `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — add a section (header =
462	   provisional commit title; intro paragraph + any `###` design
463	   subsections; **no** per-file edit list — that's the commit
464	   body).
465	9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
466	   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
467	10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
468	    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
469	
470	## Sub-step Workflow

file.content:
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

### Pre-commit checklist

At cycle *start* (before the version bump): (a) backfill the
previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
commit's URL + full SHA — see
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references); (b) if
this cycle picks up a `## Todo` item (or re-scopes `## In
Progress` to absorb one), delete that `## Todo` entry — see
[Versioning](#versioning).

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
   Versioning multi-step section.)
7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
   move the entry from `## In Progress` to `## Done`.
8. Update `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — add a section (header =
   provisional commit title; intro paragraph + any `###` design
   subsections; **no** per-file edit list — that's the commit
   body).
9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

## Sub-step Workflow

content.text: Now the CLAUDE.md `### Versioning` rewrite:

input.old_string:
A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **deletes that
`## Todo` entry in the same commit** (the version-bump / plan
commit). `## In Progress` is the sole record of that work until
close-out, when it moves to `## Done`; a cycle that merges
several entangled `## Todo` items deletes all of them. A `## Todo`
entry that duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
bug.
input.new_string:
A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **moves that
entry into `## In Progress` in the same commit** (the
version-bump / plan commit):

- **Pickup:** the entry's text moves out of `## Todo` and into
  `## In Progress` — its overview and to-do list, followed by
  the plan ladder — so the section is self-describing, not a
  bare ladder. The chores opener **mirrors** that content so
  the cycle stays readable once `## In Progress` is cleared.
- **Close-out:** the item is removed from `## In Progress` and
  a one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) is added to
  `## Done`.

`## In Progress` is the sole live record of the work between
those points. A cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo`
items moves all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates
current `## In Progress` work is a process bug.

For a single-step or squash close-out cycle, pickup and
close-out both land before the one commit, so the populated
`## In Progress` is a working-copy/review artifact that never
reaches committed history — expected; the chores opener is
then the durable capture.

+current `## In Progress` work is a process bug.

+`## In Progress` is a working-copy/review artifact that never
+reaches committed history — expected; the chores opener is
+then the durable capture.

### Bot-repo cadence under squash close-out

CLAUDE.md's `.claude` cadence rule commits `.claude` per
sub-step, alongside the app repo; it explicitly rejected
"accumulate across the cycle, commit once at close-out" to
preserve the option of promoting any sub-step to its own
push. A **squash** close-out voids that rationale — no
sub-step is promoted — so this cycle ran the bot side as a
single node:

- `.claude` is one working-copy node for the whole cycle,
  described and committed once at close-out. Its change ID
  is stable from cycle start — working-copy snapshots,
  `jj describe`, and the finalize `jj commit` all preserve
  it.
- All three app sub-step commits carry the same
  `ochid: /.claude/<id>` — that stable `.claude` change ID.
- The app stack squashes downward into node 1, so node 1's
  change ID survives as the final `0.56.0` commit. Both
  ochid directions are known at cycle start; the squash
  needs no ochid fixup.

The honest rule: per-sub-step `.claude` commits for a
keep-separate close-out, one `.claude` node for a squash
close-out. Codifying that in CLAUDE.md's `.claude` cadence
section is a follow-up — see `## Todo`.

1) Prepare and Plan, commit + desc
2) Do work, commit + desc
3) Prepare close-out, commit, squash 1..3, desc + push

1) Prepare and Plan, commit + desc + push
2) Do work, commit + desc + push, loop to 2)
3) Prepare close-out, commit + desc + push

content.text:
You're right — structurally they're the same protocol. Let me sharpen *why*, because I think it points at a real cleanup.

## The two are the same 3-phase protocol

Plan → Work(×N) → Close-out. What actually differs is **two independent settings**, and the current taxonomy bundles them into one label:

- **Decomposition** — how many local Work commits: 1, or N.
- **Remote shape** — what lands on `origin`: every commit pushed as it's made (N remote commits), or held and squashed to 1, pushed once.

"Single-step" and "multi-step" each pick *both* settings at once, as a bundle. That's why "single-step with 3 sub-steps" sounds self-contradictory — it is, under the current vocabulary. What we're actually running is a **multi-step ladder (`0.56.0-0/-1/-2`) with pushes withheld and a squash close-out**. Calling it "single-step" only describes the *outcome* (one commit on `main`), not the work.

## Is it simpler?

Honest answer: it gives a simpler **remote history** (one commit per logical change, no half-finished dev markers) and simpler **review** (no mid-cycle remote state to reason about) — at the cost of the squash plus the `.claude`-cadence / ochid bookkeeping we had to work out. It is *not* simpler *process*; it's the same work, differently optimized. For a trivial cycle it's pure dogfooding cost, which you already accepted.

## The concrete consequence for what you're reviewing

This is exactly why the last paragraph of my CLAUDE.md rewrite reads awkwardly:

> "For a single-step or squash close-out cycle, pickup and close-out both land before the one commit..."

That paragraph *entangles* the pickup rule with push/squash policy — the very entanglement you just spotted. **I recommend dropping it entirely.** The `**Pickup:**` bullet already says "the chores opener **mirrors** that content so the cycle stays readable once `## In Progress` is cleared" — that states the durable-capture mechanism *uniformly*, for every cycle shape. Whether `## In Progress` churn itself survives in pushed history is a push/squash concern, not part of the pickup rule, and doesn't need saying.

So the In-Progress rule stays uniform: pickup populates, close-out clears, chores opener mirrors. No cycle-type carve-out.

## The bigger idea

Re-basing CLAUDE.md's taxonomy onto those two axes (decomposition × remote-shape) instead of single/multi/sub-step is genuinely worth doing — but it's a separate cycle, not this one (this cycle = In-Progress codification). I'd capture it as a `## Todo` item.

How do you want to proceed — drop that paragraph and finish node 2 as planned, and add a todo for the taxonomy rework?

content.text:
Yes, makes sense — the nomenclature is good. Let me lock the model so we're aligned before I touch CLAUDE.md:

**The cycle protocol — three phases:**
1. **Preparation** — version bump; pickup (`## Todo` item → `## In Progress`); chores opener (mirrors `## In Progress`).
2. **Work-N** — the implementation; 1..N commits.
3. **Close-out** — `## In Progress` item → `## Done` one-liner; finalize the chores section.

**Two modes — how the cycle reaches the remote:**
- **multi-step** — commit + push at the end of Preparation, every Work commit, and Close-out. N+2 commits on `origin`.
- **single-step** — Preparation and Work commits stay local; Close-out squashes the N+2 stack into one and pushes once.

Preparation and Close-out bracket the cycle, so "the work" is exactly Work-N between them — your identifiability point. (One naming snag: "single-step" now produces one commit via *squash*, not by being one step. `Squashed` / `Incremental` describe what actually differs. Your call — I'll note it, not insist.)

**This rescopes our cycle** from "codify In-Progress pickup" to "refine the cycle protocol" — In-Progress pickup becomes one part. That's a legitimate `## In Progress` re-scope (the rule we're writing literally allows it). I'll update `## In Progress`, the chores opener, and the cycle title to the broader scope as part of the work.

**The one decision I need before editing** — the existing `## Sub-step Workflow` section (~200 lines). A flat **Work-N** makes the sub-step / sub-sub-step *version nesting* (`X.Y.Z-N.M`, `X.Y.Z-N.M-K`) redundant: "more granularity" just means "more Work commits." Two ways:

- **(a) Flatten** — collapse single/multi/sub/sub-sub into the one Prep/Work-N/Close-out protocol + two modes; delete the version-nesting; re-home the still-good mechanical content (commit-first review model, per-commit cargo cycle, ochid trailers, reviewing committed revisions) under it. Big clarity win, rewrites a large section.
- **(b) Keep the nesting** — bolt the phase names on top, leave `X.Y.Z-N.M` as-is. Less churn, redundancy stays.

I recommend **(a)** — it's the actual simplification you're after — but it's a big rewrite, so I want your call first.

**Ladder** stays `0.56.0-0/-1/-2` → squash `0.56.0`: node 2 (`0.56.0-1`, the current working copy) becomes the Work commit that restructures `### Versioning` + `## Sub-step Workflow`; node 3 stays Close-out.

Confirm (a) or (b)?

content.text:
Good refinements. Going through them:

**single-push / multi-push** — agree. "Push" is the right axis word, and it kills the "single-step that's actually ≥3 steps" contradiction.

**Keep nesting** — agreed, dropping my flatten idea. Subdividing a Work item that turns out bigger than planned is a real need, and nesting handles it without renumbering siblings.

**single-push without squash** — your read holds together. The clean way to state it is two axes:
- *push timing*: incremental (multi-push) vs once (single-push)
- *squash*: yes / no

`.claude` commits once per push (it captures session state at the push point), so:
- **multi-push** → per-phase `.claude` commits, paired 1:1 with code commits, one ochid each.
- **single-push** → one `.claude` node for the whole cycle.
  - *+squash*: code collapses to one commit → 1:1.
  - *no squash*: code keeps N+2 commits → N:1; the `.claude` description carries a **list** of ochids, every code commit carries the same single `.claude` ochid. Exactly as you said.

So single-push-no-squash has multi-push's remote shape but single-push's `.claude` structure — a coherent corner, worth documenting, probably not the common case.

**Numbering — one push-back, one honesty note.**

*Push-back: drop the dot-vs-dash split.* Your examples use `.` for a simple subdivision (`-3.1`) and `-` for a complex one (`-3-0`, `-3-1`) — same concept, a nested work item, with two notations chosen by whether the level has a Preparation. That's fragile: a simple subdivision that later needs a Preparation forces a `-3.1 → -3-1` renumber.

Use **one separator, `.`**, everywhere (semver mandates `-` once at the start of the prerelease, then `.` for every level after). Rule generalizes yours: **a trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation** — at any depth:

```
x.y.z-0       Preparation (top)
x.y.z-1..n    Work (top)
x.y.z         Close-out (top)

x.y.z-3.0     Preparation of the -3 sub-cycle
x.y.z-3.1..n  Work
x.y.z-3       Close-out of -3

x.y.z-3.1.0   Preparation of the -3.1 sub-sub-cycle ...
```

A simple subdivision is just `-3.1, -3.2, -3`. If it later needs a Preparation, add `-3.0` — siblings untouched. One rule, nests to any depth. (This changes the existing sub-sub notation `-3.4-1` → `-3.4.1`; going-forward only, past commits keep their numbers.)

*Honesty note on ordering.* A **nested close-out sorts before its children**: semver-wise `x.y.z-3 < x.y.z-3.0 < x.y.z-3.1` — a prefix always precedes its extensions. So within a nested sub-cycle the version order isn't chronological. It's cosmetic (nothing depends on it; the one real guarantee — bare `X.Y.Z` outranks every dev version — still holds). But note: CLAUDE.md *currently claims* `0.6.0-3.4-1 < 0.6.0-3.4-2 < 0.6.0-3.4`, and that tail is already false — the close-out `-3.4` sorts *first*. The rewrite should correct it: "nested close-outs sort before their children; the only ordering guarantee is final `X.Y.Z` > all dev versions."

Confirm the consistent-`.` scheme and I'll fold all of this into the CLAUDE.md rewrite.
