content.content:
1	# Forking and multi-user coordination
2	
3	Captures design discussion about two related but separable
4	concerns:
5	
6	1. **Forking** a dual-repo workspace — working on an older
7	   code-side base while keeping the bot side append-only.
8	2. **Multi-user collaboration** on the same dual-repo —
9	   multiple humans, multiple bot sessions, code-side merges.
10	
11	Both surface the same underlying tension: the code repo has
12	the classic git/jj DAG (branches, merges, rebases), while the
13	bot repo is fundamentally an append-only journal of
14	conversations that should never be rewritten.
15	
16	For the *content* of the bot repo (vendor identity, format
17	versioning, multi-bot conventions, vendor-subdir directory
18	layout), see the companion file
19	[`bot-data-formats.md`](bot-data-formats.md).
20	
21	This file is forward-looking: most of what it describes is
22	the bot thinks, not implemented. Treat as a design reference,
23	not a status doc.
24	
25	## Forking with the bot side staying linear
26	
27	### The asymmetric situation
28	
29	When work needs to happen on an older code-side base — e.g.,
30	0.41.1 work branched off 0.41.0 while 0.42.0 is in flight on
31	main — the code repo can fork without difficulty. That's a
32	normal git/jj branch.
33	
34	The bot repo can't fork the same way: each session is a linear
35	conversation, and rewriting an existing session line would
36	falsify the historical record. The conversation that happened
37	is the conversation that happened; it doesn't get re-played
38	from a different branch base.
39	
40	### chid stability across rebase
41	
42	jj's two-id model is built for this scenario. Each commit has
43	both:
44	
45	- `commit_id` — git SHA, changes on rewrite.
46	- `change_id` — stable across `describe` / `rebase` /
47	  `squash-into-self`.
48	
49	`ochid:` trailers carry change_ids. So when a code-side branch
50	later rebases on top of another branch, change_ids are
51	preserved and ochid trailers in both directions still resolve.
52	
53	The bot thinks the only operations that break this are
54	`jj squash` (folding two commits into one — collapses two
55	change_ids into one) and `jj abandon` (removing a commit —
56	loses the change_id entirely). Pure rebase is safe.
57	
58	### Why not time-based linkers
59	
60	Time isn't a stronger primitive than change_id:
61	
62	- Clock skew between processes / repos breaks ordering.
63	- Time still doesn't survive rewrites any better than
64	  change_id does — you'd still need a stable identifier baked
65	  into the trailer that matches some property of the
66	  counterpart commit.
67	- change_id is jj's intended stability primitive — that's
68	  literally the job it exists to do.
69	
70	If the change_id scheme ever shows weakness, the bot thinks
71	the answer is jj-side hygiene (don't `squash`-collapse during
72	rebase) rather than switching primitives.
73	
74	### Partner bookmarks
75	
76	The chosen technique for forking the code side while keeping
77	the bot side linear: **partner bookmarks**.
78	
79	When a code-side branch needs to advance independently, create
80	a bookmark of the same name on the bot side starting at the
81	*current* bot HEAD — not at the historical bot commit paired
82	with the code-side branch base. The two bookmarks share a name
83	and advance together via `vc-x1 push`, but each carries its
84	own repo's state.
85	
86	Other names considered and rejected:
87	
88	- **mirror** — implies symmetric content. The bookmarks are
89	  not mirrors; they hold different content with no
90	  point-by-point correspondence.
91	- **tandem** — captures cooperation, but slightly less common
92	  in software vocabulary.
93	- **paired** — bland; "pair" can imply matching halves.
94	- **disjoint** / **asymmetric** — describe a property of the
95	  relationship, not a label for the technique.
96	- **yoked** — accurate metaphor but obscure.
97	
98	**partner** won because it conveys cooperation without
99	implying duplication. Always subject to change as we live
100	with the term.
101	
102	The asymmetric ordering between code and bot history (rebased
103	main commits on the code side will eventually point at bot
104	commits that come *before* their partner-branch pairs in bot
105	history) is a cosmetic wrinkle, not a correctness problem —
106	change_ids resolve regardless of position.
107	
108	### Recovery anchor
109	
110	The original main HEAD — the commit being "branched away
111	from" — should stay put, not be rewritten or rewound. It's
112	the recovery anchor for the operation. If the partner-branch
113	work goes wrong, main is still where it was, and recovery is
114	`jj abandon` on the new branch's commits.
115	
116	In the 0.41.1 init+clone refactor, this means main stays at
117	its current 0.42.0-4 docs-capture commit (`55eadc8`) for the
118	duration of the side-branch work. That commit becomes
119	redundant after the eventual rebase — its content has been
120	re-landed on `init-clone-refactor` — and gets dropped during
121	the rebase rather than being rewound now.
122	
123	## Multi-user collaboration
124	
125	The bot thinks the same model — linear forest on the bot
126	side, multi-trailer reconciliation on the code side —
127	generalizes naturally to multi-user.
128	
129	### Bot repo as a forest of linear chains
130	
131	Each session is one append-only chain. The bot repo is a
132	forest of those chains; never merged. No bot-side merge
133	commits at all. Code-side has a DAG (merges, branches);
134	bot-side is a forest. The mapping is the multi-ochid trailer
135	set on each code commit, fanning out to the contributing
136	chain tips.
137	
138	The bot thinks this is cleaner than "bot side mirrors code
139	side topology" because:
140	
141	- Each conversation is a wire-format record of what was
142	  actually said. Merging two conversations into one commit
143	  would falsify that record.
144	- "Never rewrite bot commits" becomes an enforceable rule —
145	  change_ids on the bot side are automatically stable.
146	- Storage and sync stay simple — appending only, no merge
147	  conflict resolution on the bot side ever.
148	
149	### Multi-line ochid trailer
150	
151	Standard git trailer convention allows multiple values for
152	the same key. Just emit `ochid:` once per pointer:
153	
154	```
155	ochid: /.claude/abc123
156	ochid: /.claude/def456
157	ochid: /.claude/ghi789
158	```
159	
160	Single-pointer commits stay backward compatible — they're
161	just the N=1 case. No version bump needed; existing parsers
162	either already accept repeated trailers or are easy to
163	extend.
164	
165	### When multi-ochid actually applies
166	
167	- **Code-side merge with no extra bot work** — N ochids =
168	  the merge parents' contributing tip change_ids (one per
169	  parent). Possibly with de-duplication if parents share
170	  ancestry.
171	- **Code-side merge with conflict-resolution session** —
172	  N parents' ochids + the resolution session's tip ochid.
173	  If user A and user B merge into M and user C resolves the
174	  conflict, M has 3 ochids: A's tip, B's tip, C's session
175	  tip.
176	- **Single-user single-session** — still 1 ochid. Same as
177	  today.
178	- **Code rebase** — ochids unchanged (change_ids are
179	  stable). Same as today. Multi-user rebases work the same
180	  as single-user.
181	
182	### Per-user vs shared bot repo
183	
184	Open question worth deciding before code lands.
185	
186	- **Shared bot repo** (today's model extended): one
187	  `.claude/` repo holds all sessions across all users. Push
188	  from any user appends a chain. Pros: viewer sees
189	  everything in one place; cross-references resolve locally.
190	  Cons: every user pulls every other user's session data on
191	  sync — could be heavy. Permissions are coarse.
192	- **Per-user bot repos**: each user has their own
193	  `.claude-<user>/` repo, ochid trailers carry a user
194	  qualifier (`ochid: /.claude-alice/abc123`). Pros:
195	  lightweight sync, natural permissions. Cons: viewer needs
196	  to know all the bot-repo URLs to show full context;
197	  offline viewing breaks if you don't have the relevant
198	  user's repo.
199	
200	The bot thinks **shared** is the default with a per-project
201	escape hatch — most teams want the cross-references to
202	"just work." The qualifier in ochid (the path component
203	after the leading `/`) already encodes either model:
204	`/.claude/<chid>` for shared, `/.claude-alice/<chid>` for
205	per-user. The trailer format itself is forward-compatible.
206	
207	### Per-user bot repos via URL-shaped ochid
208	
209	Per-user bot repos become practical for distributed
210	projects (open-source, multi-org) when ochid trailers can
211	qualify by URL rather than only workspace-relative path.
212	This generalizes the per-user model in the section above
213	from "co-located teams sharing a workspace" to "anyone on
214	the public internet."
215	
216	**Trailer shape.** Existing path form is
217	workspace-root-relative (`/.claude/<chid>` for shared,
218	`/.claude-alice/<chid>` for per-user-in-shared-workspace).
219	URL form generalizes the qualifier to a remote repo:
220	
221	```
222	ochid: https://github.com/alice/bot-sessions#<chid>
223	ochid: git@github.com:alice/bot-sessions.git#<chid>
224	```
225	
226	Parser dispatch is one rule: if the qualifier parses as a
227	URL, fetch over the network; otherwise resolve relative
228	to workspace root. Existing path-form trailers stay the
229	N=1 backward-compatible case. No schema versioning
230	needed.
231	
232	**Link rot is the load-bearing problem.** A contributor
233	can publish their bot repo, get merged, then later make
234	it private or delete it. The ochid URL 404s; bot context
235	is lost. The bot thinks this is a real-but-not-
236	catastrophic leak — the commit's code and
237	`Signed-off-by:` line remain authoritative; bot context
238	is a nice-to-have for understanding patch development,
239	not a load-bearing semantic. Direct analogue: dead URLs
240	in academic citations, mailing-list links in older
241	kernel commit messages. Annoying for archaeology, not
242	fatal.
243	
244	**Mitigations, in order of practical adoption:**
245	
246	1. **Project-side mirroring at merge time.** When a
247	   maintainer accepts a PR, project tooling fetches the
248	   contributor's bot repo at the referenced chid and
249	   mirrors to a project-namespace archive (e.g.
250	   `bot-archives/<contributor>-<date>-<chid>.jsonl`).
251	   Future ochid resolution falls back to the mirror if
252	   the original URL 404s. The bot thinks this is the
253	   only mitigation most projects would actually adopt —
254	   cheap, obvious upside, no friction for contributors.
255	   Same pattern as LKML's permanent archive.
256	2. **Cryptographic stapling.** Embed a hash of the bot
257	   repo's tip at submission time:
258	   `ochid: <url>#<chid>;sha256=<hash>`. Tamper-detection
259	   stays sound even if retrieval fails; doesn't recover
260	   data. Adds maintenance burden to trailer parsing.
261	3. **CI-enforced live ochid at merge.** Project CI
262	   fetches the URL, verifies it resolves with parseable
263	   content, before allowing merge. Doesn't prevent
264	   later lock/delete but ensures the merge moment was
265	   sound. Contentious in open-source culture (gates
266	   contributors on infra they don't own).
267	
268	**Opt-out via project policy.** A no-bot-context commit
269	isn't a leak — it's a project policy choice. A project
270	that wants bot transparency mandates each commit carry
271	≥1 `ochid:` trailer or an explicit
272	`No-bot-context: <reason>` trailer. Enforceable in CI,
273	identical pattern to existing `Signed-off-by:`.
274	Contributors who want privacy decline that project's
275	contribution norms; their commits go elsewhere or omit
276	the trailer where the project allows.
277	
278	**Cross-bot interop bonus.** Once URL-shaped, the
279	trailer doesn't have to point at a Claude Code repo.
280	Codex, Cursor, Aider, custom in-house tools — anyone
281	whose bot leaves a publishable session record can host
282	their own bot repo and emit ochid trailers in the same
283	format. The trailer becomes "where to find the AI
284	session that produced this commit," not "where to find
285	the Claude Code session." Strictly bigger ecosystem
286	play with no extra design cost.
287	
288	**Implication for shared-central scaling.** URL-shaped
289	per-user repos potentially short-circuit the
290	size/threshold problem documented below. Each
291	contributor pays for their own bot repo; project pays
292	for an opt-in mirror archive sized per its policy. The
293	"50 devs share one ~15G bot repo" problem becomes "50
294	devs each maintain their own ~300M bot repo, project
295	mirrors selectively."
296	
297	Trade-off: shared-central keeps cross-references local
298	and fast; per-user-via-URL needs a network fetch (or a
299	warm mirror) to resolve any non-local trailer. Pick by
300	project shape — small co-located teams favor shared;
301	distributed open-source favors per-user-via-URL.
302	
303	### Bot-side commit immutability
304	
305	Codify "never rewrite bot commits" as a hard rule. The
306	reason: change_ids on the bot side need to be permanently
307	stable for ochid trailers from the code side to resolve
308	forever. Even `jj describe` (description-only) changes the
309	commit_id, which doesn't break change_ids but does signal
310	that bot commits are mutable in a way they shouldn't be.
311	
312	Trade: any commit-level metadata fix has to be done by
313	appending a new commit, not amending the existing one.
314	Awkward but correct.
315	
316	### Bot-repo size and scaling thresholds
317	
318	Concrete measurement on a single-developer vc-x1 project
319	(2026-05-07, after `cargo clean`):
320	
321	- Code repo (excluding `.claude`): ~4M
322	- Bot repo (`.claude`): 311M
323	- Ratio: ~78:1 bot-to-code
324	
325	Cause: every Claude Code session writes a UUID-named
326	`.jsonl` file. Content is mostly unique per session —
327	git/jj packs each file's append history well, but
328	cross-file dedup is weak. So bot-repo bytes scale roughly
329	linearly with total session count.
330	
331	Linear extrapolation to shared-central multi-user (the bot
332	thinks this is a reasonable upper bound; actual savings
333	from cross-session dedup are small):
334	
335	- 5 devs: ~1.5G
336	- 20 devs: ~6G
337	- 50 devs: ~15G
338	
339	Rough operational thresholds:
340	
341	- **≤ ~10 users:** shared-central is fine.
342	- **~10-50 users:** shared-central starts hurting
343	  (multi-minute initial clone; noticeable `vc-x1 sync`
344	  pulls).
345	- **> ~50 users or > ~10G bot repo:** mitigations become
346	  necessary.
347	
348	### Monotonic-growth asymmetry
349	
350	Bot-repo size only grows. Once a session jsonl is
351	committed, the bot-side immutability rule keeps it
352	forever. This is by design (historical record), but it
353	means the bot repo has no analog of `cargo clean`:
354	code-repo working bytes can be reclaimed (build artifacts
355	come back on `cargo build`); bot-repo bytes are permanent.
356	
357	Sizing assumptions for shared-central scaling are a
358	one-way ratchet, not a steady-state cost.
359	
360	### Mitigation menu (when thresholds approach)
361	
362	None worth building preemptively. Listed so trigger points
363	have known responses:
364	
365	1. **Bot-repo pruning/archiving.** Move closed-out
366	   sessions (older than N months, or tied to commits no
367	   longer in any active branch) to an archive repo.
368	   Active bot repo stays small; archive fetched on demand
369	   when older history is referenced. The bot thinks this
370	   is the cheapest big win — recent sessions are the hot
371	   set, old ones are cold.
372	2. **Sparse fetch by trailer-referenced sessions.** When
373	   fetching the code repo, derive the set of bot-side
374	   change_ids referenced by ochids in the fetched range
375	   and fetch only those bot commits. Requires git/jj
376	   sparse-fetch + a trailer-walking step. Heavier to
377	   build but decouples clone cost from bot-repo size.
378	3. **Per-user bot repos.** The structural alternative —
379	   see "Per-user vs shared bot repo" above. Viable when
380	   shared-central pruning/sparse-fetch isn't enough.
381	4. **LFS for `.jsonl`.** Probably overkill — LFS targets
382	   binary blobs, not append-only text. Listed only
383	   because it's a known git escape hatch.
384	
385	### Tracking trigger
386	
387	Concretely measurable: the 311M baseline is the current
388	benchmark. When the shared-central bot repo crosses ~1G
389	or starts dominating clone time noticeably, that's the
390	design trigger for pruning. User count alone is not the
391	right signal; bot-repo size is.
392	
393	## Future-direction notes
394	
395	Two alternatives to the partner-bookmark technique that may
396	be worth considering once we live with the current scheme:
397	
398	### Always keep the bot repo on main
399	
400	Instead of creating a partner bookmark on the bot side for
401	each code-side branch, the bot side could be locked to a
402	single bookmark — `main` — for the entire lifetime of the
403	project. Every push would advance bot main, regardless of
404	what bookmark the code side is on.
405	
406	The bot thinks this aligns with "bot history is one linear
407	journal" more strongly than partner bookmarks do. Partner
408	bookmarks introduce *parallel* bot bookmarks even though the
409	bot history under them is still linear; an always-main
410	discipline removes that parallelism entirely.
411	
412	Trade: requires push / finalize / sync to support asymmetric
413	bookmark names per side (see next note).
414	
415	### Multi-bookmark-name support in push / finalize / sync
416	
417	Today, `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` advances the same bookmark
418	name on both code and bot sides. The "always-on-main"
419	discipline above (and other asymmetric workflows) requires
420	push / finalize / sync to take separate bookmark arguments
421	per side, e.g.:
422	
423	```
424	vc-x1 push --code-bookmark init-clone-refactor --bot-bookmark main
425	vc-x1 push -B init-clone-refactor              # bot defaults to main
426	vc-x1 push init-clone-refactor                  # both, today's behavior
427	```
428	
429	The bot thinks the right shape lands once `--scope` is
430	fully wired across the dual-repo commands — `--scope`
431	already handles "which side does this command apply to,"
432	and adding per-side bookmark arguments is a natural
433	extension.
434	
435	## Open questions worth thinking about
436	
437	- **Conflict resolution sessions: how do they get
438	  authored?** When user C resolves a merge conflict, does
439	  their bot session start a fresh chain rooted "from
440	  nowhere," or branch off one of the contributing chains?
441	  The chain-rooting question affects how the viewer renders
442	  it.
443	- **De-duplication on octopus merges.** If parents share
444	  ancestry, you'll get redundant ochids. Trim to unique
445	  tips? Keep all? The bot thinks trim, but worth deciding.
446	- **Bot-side bookmark scheme.** Per-session bookmark?
447	  Per-user-per-feature? Naming convention matters because
448	  bookmarks are how `jj git push` decides what to send.
449	- **Viewer concerns.** "Show me the sessions that
450	  contributed to this commit" is straightforward (read the
451	  trailers, walk back in each chain to the relevant range).
452	  "Show me the code commits this session contributed to" is
453	  the inverse and needs an index, since change_ids don't
454	  carry forward references. Probably build the index from a
455	  one-time scan of code-side trailers.
456	- **Migration story for existing repos** with single-ochid
457	  trailers — they're already valid in the multi-ochid
458	  scheme (N=1 case), so probably no migration needed. Worth
459	  confirming on the parser side.
460

file.content:
# Forking and multi-user coordination

Captures design discussion about two related but separable
concerns:

1. **Forking** a dual-repo workspace — working on an older
   code-side base while keeping the bot side append-only.
2. **Multi-user collaboration** on the same dual-repo —
   multiple humans, multiple bot sessions, code-side merges.

Both surface the same underlying tension: the code repo has
the classic git/jj DAG (branches, merges, rebases), while the
bot repo is fundamentally an append-only journal of
conversations that should never be rewritten.

For the *content* of the bot repo (vendor identity, format
versioning, multi-bot conventions, vendor-subdir directory
layout), see the companion file
[`bot-data-formats.md`](bot-data-formats.md).

This file is forward-looking: most of what it describes is
the bot thinks, not implemented. Treat as a design reference,
not a status doc.

## Forking with the bot side staying linear

### The asymmetric situation

When work needs to happen on an older code-side base — e.g.,
0.41.1 work branched off 0.41.0 while 0.42.0 is in flight on
main — the code repo can fork without difficulty. That's a
normal git/jj branch.

The bot repo can't fork the same way: each session is a linear
conversation, and rewriting an existing session line would
falsify the historical record. The conversation that happened
is the conversation that happened; it doesn't get re-played
from a different branch base.

### chid stability across rebase

jj's two-id model is built for this scenario. Each commit has
both:

- `commit_id` — git SHA, changes on rewrite.
- `change_id` — stable across `describe` / `rebase` /
  `squash-into-self`.

`ochid:` trailers carry change_ids. So when a code-side branch
later rebases on top of another branch, change_ids are
preserved and ochid trailers in both directions still resolve.

The bot thinks the only operations that break this are
`jj squash` (folding two commits into one — collapses two
change_ids into one) and `jj abandon` (removing a commit —
loses the change_id entirely). Pure rebase is safe.

### Why not time-based linkers

Time isn't a stronger primitive than change_id:

- Clock skew between processes / repos breaks ordering.
- Time still doesn't survive rewrites any better than
  change_id does — you'd still need a stable identifier baked
  into the trailer that matches some property of the
  counterpart commit.
- change_id is jj's intended stability primitive — that's
  literally the job it exists to do.

If the change_id scheme ever shows weakness, the bot thinks
the answer is jj-side hygiene (don't `squash`-collapse during
rebase) rather than switching primitives.

### Partner bookmarks

The chosen technique for forking the code side while keeping
the bot side linear: **partner bookmarks**.

When a code-side branch needs to advance independently, create
a bookmark of the same name on the bot side starting at the
*current* bot HEAD — not at the historical bot commit paired
with the code-side branch base. The two bookmarks share a name
and advance together via `vc-x1 push`, but each carries its
own repo's state.

Other names considered and rejected:

- **mirror** — implies symmetric content. The bookmarks are
  not mirrors; they hold different content with no
  point-by-point correspondence.
- **tandem** — captures cooperation, but slightly less common
  in software vocabulary.
- **paired** — bland; "pair" can imply matching halves.
- **disjoint** / **asymmetric** — describe a property of the
  relationship, not a label for the technique.
- **yoked** — accurate metaphor but obscure.

**partner** won because it conveys cooperation without
implying duplication. Always subject to change as we live
with the term.

The asymmetric ordering between code and bot history (rebased
main commits on the code side will eventually point at bot
commits that come *before* their partner-branch pairs in bot
history) is a cosmetic wrinkle, not a correctness problem —
change_ids resolve regardless of position.

### Recovery anchor

The original main HEAD — the commit being "branched away
from" — should stay put, not be rewritten or rewound. It's
the recovery anchor for the operation. If the partner-branch
work goes wrong, main is still where it was, and recovery is
`jj abandon` on the new branch's commits.

In the 0.41.1 init+clone refactor, this means main stays at
its current 0.42.0-4 docs-capture commit (`55eadc8`) for the
duration of the side-branch work. That commit becomes
redundant after the eventual rebase — its content has been
re-landed on `init-clone-refactor` — and gets dropped during
the rebase rather than being rewound now.

## Multi-user collaboration

The bot thinks the same model — linear forest on the bot
side, multi-trailer reconciliation on the code side —
generalizes naturally to multi-user.

### Bot repo as a forest of linear chains

Each session is one append-only chain. The bot repo is a
forest of those chains; never merged. No bot-side merge
commits at all. Code-side has a DAG (merges, branches);
bot-side is a forest. The mapping is the multi-ochid trailer
set on each code commit, fanning out to the contributing
chain tips.

The bot thinks this is cleaner than "bot side mirrors code
side topology" because:

- Each conversation is a wire-format record of what was
  actually said. Merging two conversations into one commit
  would falsify that record.
- "Never rewrite bot commits" becomes an enforceable rule —
  change_ids on the bot side are automatically stable.
- Storage and sync stay simple — appending only, no merge
  conflict resolution on the bot side ever.

### Multi-line ochid trailer

Standard git trailer convention allows multiple values for
the same key. Just emit `ochid:` once per pointer:

```
ochid: /.claude/abc123
ochid: /.claude/def456
ochid: /.claude/ghi789
```

Single-pointer commits stay backward compatible — they're
just the N=1 case. No version bump needed; existing parsers
either already accept repeated trailers or are easy to
extend.

### When multi-ochid actually applies

- **Code-side merge with no extra bot work** — N ochids =
  the merge parents' contributing tip change_ids (one per
  parent). Possibly with de-duplication if parents share
  ancestry.
- **Code-side merge with conflict-resolution session** —
  N parents' ochids + the resolution session's tip ochid.
  If user A and user B merge into M and user C resolves the
  conflict, M has 3 ochids: A's tip, B's tip, C's session
  tip.
- **Single-user single-session** — still 1 ochid. Same as
  today.
- **Code rebase** — ochids unchanged (change_ids are
  stable). Same as today. Multi-user rebases work the same
  as single-user.

### Per-user vs shared bot repo

Open question worth deciding before code lands.

- **Shared bot repo** (today's model extended): one
  `.claude/` repo holds all sessions across all users. Push
  from any user appends a chain. Pros: viewer sees
  everything in one place; cross-references resolve locally.
  Cons: every user pulls every other user's session data on
  sync — could be heavy. Permissions are coarse.
- **Per-user bot repos**: each user has their own
  `.claude-<user>/` repo, ochid trailers carry a user
  qualifier (`ochid: /.claude-alice/abc123`). Pros:
  lightweight sync, natural permissions. Cons: viewer needs
  to know all the bot-repo URLs to show full context;
  offline viewing breaks if you don't have the relevant
  user's repo.

The bot thinks **shared** is the default with a per-project
escape hatch — most teams want the cross-references to
"just work." The qualifier in ochid (the path component
after the leading `/`) already encodes either model:
`/.claude/<chid>` for shared, `/.claude-alice/<chid>` for
per-user. The trailer format itself is forward-compatible.

### Per-user bot repos via URL-shaped ochid

Per-user bot repos become practical for distributed
projects (open-source, multi-org) when ochid trailers can
qualify by URL rather than only workspace-relative path.
This generalizes the per-user model in the section above
from "co-located teams sharing a workspace" to "anyone on
the public internet."

**Trailer shape.** Existing path form is
workspace-root-relative (`/.claude/<chid>` for shared,
`/.claude-alice/<chid>` for per-user-in-shared-workspace).
URL form generalizes the qualifier to a remote repo:

```
ochid: https://github.com/alice/bot-sessions#<chid>
ochid: git@github.com:alice/bot-sessions.git#<chid>
```

Parser dispatch is one rule: if the qualifier parses as a
URL, fetch over the network; otherwise resolve relative
to workspace root. Existing path-form trailers stay the
N=1 backward-compatible case. No schema versioning
needed.

**Link rot is the load-bearing problem.** A contributor
can publish their bot repo, get merged, then later make
it private or delete it. The ochid URL 404s; bot context
is lost. The bot thinks this is a real-but-not-
catastrophic leak — the commit's code and
`Signed-off-by:` line remain authoritative; bot context
is a nice-to-have for understanding patch development,
not a load-bearing semantic. Direct analogue: dead URLs
in academic citations, mailing-list links in older
kernel commit messages. Annoying for archaeology, not
fatal.

**Mitigations, in order of practical adoption:**

1. **Project-side mirroring at merge time.** When a
   maintainer accepts a PR, project tooling fetches the
   contributor's bot repo at the referenced chid and
   mirrors to a project-namespace archive (e.g.
   `bot-archives/<contributor>-<date>-<chid>.jsonl`).
   Future ochid resolution falls back to the mirror if
   the original URL 404s. The bot thinks this is the
   only mitigation most projects would actually adopt —
   cheap, obvious upside, no friction for contributors.
   Same pattern as LKML's permanent archive.
2. **Cryptographic stapling.** Embed a hash of the bot
   repo's tip at submission time:
   `ochid: <url>#<chid>;sha256=<hash>`. Tamper-detection
   stays sound even if retrieval fails; doesn't recover
   data. Adds maintenance burden to trailer parsing.
3. **CI-enforced live ochid at merge.** Project CI
   fetches the URL, verifies it resolves with parseable
   content, before allowing merge. Doesn't prevent
   later lock/delete but ensures the merge moment was
   sound. Contentious in open-source culture (gates
   contributors on infra they don't own).

**Opt-out via project policy.** A no-bot-context commit
isn't a leak — it's a project policy choice. A project
that wants bot transparency mandates each commit carry
≥1 `ochid:` trailer or an explicit
`No-bot-context: <reason>` trailer. Enforceable in CI,
identical pattern to existing `Signed-off-by:`.
Contributors who want privacy decline that project's
contribution norms; their commits go elsewhere or omit
the trailer where the project allows.

**Cross-bot interop bonus.** Once URL-shaped, the
trailer doesn't have to point at a Claude Code repo.
Codex, Cursor, Aider, custom in-house tools — anyone
whose bot leaves a publishable session record can host
their own bot repo and emit ochid trailers in the same
format. The trailer becomes "where to find the AI
session that produced this commit," not "where to find
the Claude Code session." Strictly bigger ecosystem
play with no extra design cost.

**Implication for shared-central scaling.** URL-shaped
per-user repos potentially short-circuit the
size/threshold problem documented below. Each
contributor pays for their own bot repo; project pays
for an opt-in mirror archive sized per its policy. The
"50 devs share one ~15G bot repo" problem becomes "50
devs each maintain their own ~300M bot repo, project
mirrors selectively."

Trade-off: shared-central keeps cross-references local
and fast; per-user-via-URL needs a network fetch (or a
warm mirror) to resolve any non-local trailer. Pick by
project shape — small co-located teams favor shared;
distributed open-source favors per-user-via-URL.

### Bot-side commit immutability

Codify "never rewrite bot commits" as a hard rule. The
reason: change_ids on the bot side need to be permanently
stable for ochid trailers from the code side to resolve
forever. Even `jj describe` (description-only) changes the
commit_id, which doesn't break change_ids but does signal
that bot commits are mutable in a way they shouldn't be.

Trade: any commit-level metadata fix has to be done by
appending a new commit, not amending the existing one.
Awkward but correct.

### Bot-repo size and scaling thresholds

Concrete measurement on a single-developer vc-x1 project
(2026-05-07, after `cargo clean`):

- Code repo (excluding `.claude`): ~4M
- Bot repo (`.claude`): 311M
- Ratio: ~78:1 bot-to-code

Cause: every Claude Code session writes a UUID-named
`.jsonl` file. Content is mostly unique per session —
git/jj packs each file's append history well, but
cross-file dedup is weak. So bot-repo bytes scale roughly
linearly with total session count.

Linear extrapolation to shared-central multi-user (the bot
thinks this is a reasonable upper bound; actual savings
from cross-session dedup are small):

- 5 devs: ~1.5G
- 20 devs: ~6G
- 50 devs: ~15G

Rough operational thresholds:

- **≤ ~10 users:** shared-central is fine.
- **~10-50 users:** shared-central starts hurting
  (multi-minute initial clone; noticeable `vc-x1 sync`
  pulls).
- **> ~50 users or > ~10G bot repo:** mitigations become
  necessary.

### Monotonic-growth asymmetry

Bot-repo size only grows. Once a session jsonl is
committed, the bot-side immutability rule keeps it
forever. This is by design (historical record), but it
means the bot repo has no analog of `cargo clean`:
code-repo working bytes can be reclaimed (build artifacts
come back on `cargo build`); bot-repo bytes are permanent.

Sizing assumptions for shared-central scaling are a
one-way ratchet, not a steady-state cost.

### Mitigation menu (when thresholds approach)

None worth building preemptively. Listed so trigger points
have known responses:

1. **Bot-repo pruning/archiving.** Move closed-out
   sessions (older than N months, or tied to commits no
   longer in any active branch) to an archive repo.
   Active bot repo stays small; archive fetched on demand
   when older history is referenced. The bot thinks this
   is the cheapest big win — recent sessions are the hot
   set, old ones are cold.
2. **Sparse fetch by trailer-referenced sessions.** When
   fetching the code repo, derive the set of bot-side
   change_ids referenced by ochids in the fetched range
   and fetch only those bot commits. Requires git/jj
   sparse-fetch + a trailer-walking step. Heavier to
   build but decouples clone cost from bot-repo size.
3. **Per-user bot repos.** The structural alternative —
   see "Per-user vs shared bot repo" above. Viable when
   shared-central pruning/sparse-fetch isn't enough.
4. **LFS for `.jsonl`.** Probably overkill — LFS targets
   binary blobs, not append-only text. Listed only
   because it's a known git escape hatch.

### Tracking trigger

Concretely measurable: the 311M baseline is the current
benchmark. When the shared-central bot repo crosses ~1G
or starts dominating clone time noticeably, that's the
design trigger for pruning. User count alone is not the
right signal; bot-repo size is.

## Future-direction notes

Two alternatives to the partner-bookmark technique that may
be worth considering once we live with the current scheme:

### Always keep the bot repo on main

Instead of creating a partner bookmark on the bot side for
each code-side branch, the bot side could be locked to a
single bookmark — `main` — for the entire lifetime of the
project. Every push would advance bot main, regardless of
what bookmark the code side is on.

The bot thinks this aligns with "bot history is one linear
journal" more strongly than partner bookmarks do. Partner
bookmarks introduce *parallel* bot bookmarks even though the
bot history under them is still linear; an always-main
discipline removes that parallelism entirely.

Trade: requires push / finalize / sync to support asymmetric
bookmark names per side (see next note).

### Multi-bookmark-name support in push / finalize / sync

Today, `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` advances the same bookmark
name on both code and bot sides. The "always-on-main"
discipline above (and other asymmetric workflows) requires
push / finalize / sync to take separate bookmark arguments
per side, e.g.:

```
vc-x1 push --code-bookmark init-clone-refactor --bot-bookmark main
vc-x1 push -B init-clone-refactor              # bot defaults to main
vc-x1 push init-clone-refactor                  # both, today's behavior
```

The bot thinks the right shape lands once `--scope` is
fully wired across the dual-repo commands — `--scope`
already handles "which side does this command apply to,"
and adding per-side bookmark arguments is a natural
extension.

## Open questions worth thinking about

- **Conflict resolution sessions: how do they get
  authored?** When user C resolves a merge conflict, does
  their bot session start a fresh chain rooted "from
  nowhere," or branch off one of the contributing chains?
  The chain-rooting question affects how the viewer renders
  it.
- **De-duplication on octopus merges.** If parents share
  ancestry, you'll get redundant ochids. Trim to unique
  tips? Keep all? The bot thinks trim, but worth deciding.
- **Bot-side bookmark scheme.** Per-session bookmark?
  Per-user-per-feature? Naming convention matters because
  bookmarks are how `jj git push` decides what to send.
- **Viewer concerns.** "Show me the sessions that
  contributed to this commit" is straightforward (read the
  trailers, walk back in each chain to the relevant range).
  "Show me the code commits this session contributed to" is
  the inverse and needs an index, since change_ids don't
  carry forward references. Probably build the index from a
  one-time scan of code-side trailers.
- **Migration story for existing repos** with single-ochid
  trailers — they're already valid in the multi-ochid
  scheme (N=1 case), so probably no migration needed. Worth
  confirming on the parser side.

input.old_string:
- **Code rebase** — ochids unchanged (change_ids are
  stable). Same as today. Multi-user rebases work the same
  as single-user.

### Per-user vs shared bot repo
input.new_string:
- **Code rebase** — ochids unchanged (change_ids are
  stable). Same as today. Multi-user rebases work the same
  as single-user.

### Staged fork integration

How a contributed cycle's worth of changes enters a
downstream repo, keeping the as-is record separate from the
integration work:

- **As-is acceptance.** A contributed set of changes lands
  first in a dedicated branch *exactly as submitted* — no
  merge, no squash, no tweak. The contributor's `ochid:`
  trailers are preserved verbatim; the branch is a faithful
  record of what was contributed.
- **Integration as separate steps.** Merging, squashing, or
  tweaking that work onto the target line happens
  afterward, as one or more separate steps on an
  intermediate branch. Each is its own cycle and may add
  its own bot session.
- **Resulting ochid set.** An integrated commit therefore
  carries a *set* of ochids — the contributor's original
  pointer(s) plus any integration-session pointer(s): the
  [multi-line ochid trailer](#multi-line-ochid-trailer)
  applied to the fork case rather than the octopus-merge
  case.

The contributing bot repos live in different locations
(different users, different remotes), so each ochid in the
set must name *which* bot repo as well as the chid. Three
representations, in increasing indirection:

- **Workspace-relative path** — `/.claude/<chid>`,
  `/.claude-alice/<chid>`. Today's form; resolves only
  repos under the local workspace root.
- **Full URL** — `https://github.com/alice/bot#<chid>`
  (see [Per-user bot repos via URL-shaped
  ochid](#per-user-bot-repos-via-url-shaped-ochid)).
  Self-contained, no external table; verbose, exposed to
  link rot.
- **`.vc-config.toml` repo index** — a tuple
  `(repo-index, chid)` where `repo-index` keys a
  repo-location table declared once in `.vc-config.toml`.
  The trailer stays short, and relocating a repo is a
  one-line config edit instead of rewriting every trailer.
  Cost: indirection — the trailer is meaningless without
  the config, so a commit inspected outside its workspace
  (a raw `git show` elsewhere) can't resolve it.

The bot thinks index and URL forms are complementary, not
rivals — index for repos the project already tracks in
`.vc-config.toml`, URL for one-off external contributors —
and a resolver accepting all three (path, URL, index) is
the one-rule dispatch the URL-shaped section already
describes, with a third arm.

### Per-user vs shared bot repo

content.text:
Done — added a `### Staged fork integration` subsection to `notes/forks-multi-user.md` (under `## Multi-user collaboration`). It captures the two genuinely new pieces:
- The staged workflow: as-is acceptance branch (ochids preserved verbatim) → merge/squash/tweak as separate cycles on an intermediate branch → integrated commit carries the resulting ochid *set*.
- The third ochid representation — `.vc-config.toml` repo-index tuple `(repo-index, chid)` — alongside the path and URL forms the file already documents, with the indirection trade-off noted.

The file already covered multi-line ochids, URL-shaped ochids, and per-user-vs-shared, so I cross-linked rather than duplicated.

## On Point 1 — one protocol

Agree, and it's the right call. There's **one protocol** — the cycle: Preparation / Work-N / Close-out, nested as needed. **push** and **squash** are not modes baked in at cycle start — they're discretionary actions:
- **push** — when you want a backup (durability) or to publish interim work (visibility).
- **squash** — when you want to tidy history, typically before a publishing push.

So "single-push / multi-push" disappear as mode names. The skeleton's "two modes" subsection becomes a short "push & squash are discretionary" note instead.

**One thing that needs your explicit confirm** — this changes an existing CLAUDE.md rule. `## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow` currently *mandates* "Run this flow after every step — dev markers land on the remote as they happen." Under discretionary push that mandate relaxes to a guideline: "push to back up or to publish." The **close-out push stays mandatory** — the cycle's result must be published — only *interim* pushes become your call. Confirm you want that mandate relaxed and I'll fold it into the rewrite.

Consequences that just follow (no decision needed): `.claude` commits once per push; an ochid set on a pushed code commit has one entry per code commit in that push.

Confirm the push-mandate relaxation and I'll do the revised CLAUDE.md rewrite as node 2.

message.content: I think the minimum rule is; review happens at each commit step and at anytime there is deviation from the plan or a question arises. The user can use ESC-ESC to get your attention if a question or review is desired early.

content.text:
Got the review rule:

- **Review at each commit step** — the commit-first model, now the *universal* rule. Not per-file, not per-push.
- I **stop for you on any deviation from the plan or a question** — no pushing through.
- **ESC-ESC** is your lever to pull a review/question forward anytime.

That also settles the push question cleanly: review attaches to **commits**, not pushes — so interim push stays discretionary (backup/publish), close-out push mandatory, and CLAUDE.md's per-file checkpoints + the "review before the commit block" ceremony collapse into the one rule. And taking your ESC-ESC point: I'll stop the per-turn "confirm?" prompts and just execute — interrupt me to steer.

Executing the rewrite now. Re-reading the current `### Versioning` + pre-commit checklist (they've shifted from my earlier node-2 edits):

content.content:
264	### Versioning
265	
266	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
267	on scope:
268	
269	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
270	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
271	- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
272	  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
273	  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.
274	
275	The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
276	starting.
277	
278	A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
279	existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **moves that
280	entry into `## In Progress` in the same commit** (the
281	version-bump / plan commit):
282	
283	- **Pickup:** the entry's text moves out of `## Todo` and into
284	  `## In Progress` — its overview and to-do list, followed by
285	  the plan ladder — so the section is self-describing, not a
286	  bare ladder. The chores opener **mirrors** that content so
287	  the cycle stays readable once `## In Progress` is cleared.
288	- **Close-out:** the item is removed from `## In Progress` and
289	  a one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) is added to
290	  `## Done`.
291	
292	`## In Progress` is the sole live record of the work between
293	those points. A cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo`
294	items moves all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates
295	current `## In Progress` work is a process bug.
296	
297	For a single-step or squash close-out cycle, pickup and
298	close-out both land before the one commit, so the populated
299	`## In Progress` is a working-copy/review artifact that never
300	reaches committed history — expected; the chores opener is
301	then the durable capture.
302	
303	For multi-step:
304	
305	1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
306	   marker.
307	2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
308	   needed).
309	3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
310	   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
311	   marker.
312	
313	Multi-step cycles place the plan ladder in
314	`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, below the picked-up item's
315	text, as a bullet list with `(done)` / `(current)` markers —
316	see the file's intro paragraph for the format. When starting a new step, the *first* edit is to mark
317	that step `(current)` in `notes/todo.md` — before any code/doc
318	work — so the In Progress view reflects what's actually
319	happening. The flip back to `(done)` is part of the pre-commit
320	checklist (item 6).
321	
322	**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
323	semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
324	component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
325	`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
326	below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
327	adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
328	doubles typing per commit.
329	
330	The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
331	amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
332	to see which commits were exploratory vs final.
333	
334	For decomposition finer than `X.Y.Z-N` — sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M`)
335	or sub-sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M-K`) — see
336	[Sub-step Workflow](#sub-step-workflow). The cycle close-out
337	choice (squash to one commit vs land each sub-step separately)
338	is made at close-out, not cycle start.
339	
340	See also [`notes/substep-protocol.md`](notes/substep-protocol.md)
341	for the full protocol — when to use, per-substep contract
342	(`cargo test --bins` is non-negotiable), navigation, the validated
343	close-out squash recipe, and recovery. Revset primitives the
344	protocol relies on (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) live in
345	[`notes/jj-revsets.md`](notes/jj-revsets.md).
346	
347	### Headings and entries that record a commit
348	
349	A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
350	commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
351	reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
352	title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
353	commit gets (see [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style)).
354	E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
355	(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
356	(0.48.0-6) [[3]]`. For a multi-step cycle the `## Done` entry
357	uses the close-out commit's title.
358	
359	This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
360	`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
361	inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
362	Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
363	default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
364	line a record up with its commit.
365	
366	A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
367	progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
368	`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
369	commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
370	for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
371	dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
372	(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).
373	
374	Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
375	the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
376	free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
377	converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
378	example.
379	
380	### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record
381	
382	A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
383	header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
384	bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
385	subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
386	structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
387	commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
388	changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
389	scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
390	for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
391	other.
392	
393	When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
394	hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
395	own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
396	wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
397	change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
398	with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
399	normal ref direction).
400	
401	**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
402	to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
403	drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
404	narrative; `Commits:` links them.
405	
406	### Chores commit references
407	
408	The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
409	line citing the git commit(s) that section records:
410	
411	```
412	## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)
413	
414	Commits: [[3]]
415	
416	<intro paragraph...>
417	```
418	
419	`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
420	[notes/README.md](notes/README.md#reference-numbering)),
421	**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
422	or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
423	literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
424	`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
425	GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
426	puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
427	40-hex SHA** in the title slot:
428	
429	```
430	[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
431	```
432	
433	- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
434	  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
435	  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
436	- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
437	  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
438	  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
439	  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.
440	
441	**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
442	written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
443	change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
444	the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
445	newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
446	commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
447	finds it.
448	
449	### Pre-commit checklist
450	
451	At cycle *start* (before the version bump): (a) backfill the
452	previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
453	commit's URL + full SHA — see
454	[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references); (b) if
455	this cycle picks up a `## Todo` item (or re-scopes `## In
456	Progress` to absorb one), move that `## Todo` entry's text
457	into `## In Progress` and mirror it in the chores opener —
458	see [Versioning](#versioning).
459	
460	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
461	
462	1. `cargo fmt`
463	2. `cargo clippy`
464	3. `cargo test`
465	4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
466	   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
467	   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
468	   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
469	5. Retest after install
470	6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
471	   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
472	   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
473	   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
474	   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
475	   Versioning multi-step section.)
476	7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
477	   remove the picked-up item from `## In Progress` and add a
478	   one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) to `## Done`.
479	8. Update `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — add a section (header =
480	   provisional commit title; intro paragraph + any `###` design
481	   subsections; **no** per-file edit list — that's the commit
482	   body).
483	9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
484	   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
485	10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
486	    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
487	
488	## Sub-step Workflow
489	
490	Larger work decomposes into named depths. The version-suffix
491	scheme nests:
492	
493	- **Single step** — one change, one commit + push + finalize.

file.content:
### Versioning

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
starting.

A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **moves that
entry into `## In Progress` in the same commit** (the
version-bump / plan commit):

- **Pickup:** the entry's text moves out of `## Todo` and into
  `## In Progress` — its overview and to-do list, followed by
  the plan ladder — so the section is self-describing, not a
  bare ladder. The chores opener **mirrors** that content so
  the cycle stays readable once `## In Progress` is cleared.
- **Close-out:** the item is removed from `## In Progress` and
  a one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) is added to
  `## Done`.

`## In Progress` is the sole live record of the work between
those points. A cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo`
items moves all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates
current `## In Progress` work is a process bug.

For a single-step or squash close-out cycle, pickup and
close-out both land before the one commit, so the populated
`## In Progress` is a working-copy/review artifact that never
reaches committed history — expected; the chores opener is
then the durable capture.

For multi-step:

1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
   marker.
2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
   needed).
3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
   marker.

Multi-step cycles place the plan ladder in
`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, below the picked-up item's
text, as a bullet list with `(done)` / `(current)` markers —
see the file's intro paragraph for the format. When starting a new step, the *first* edit is to mark
that step `(current)` in `notes/todo.md` — before any code/doc
work — so the In Progress view reflects what's actually
happening. The flip back to `(done)` is part of the pre-commit
checklist (item 6).

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
to see which commits were exploratory vs final.

For decomposition finer than `X.Y.Z-N` — sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M`)
or sub-sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M-K`) — see
[Sub-step Workflow](#sub-step-workflow). The cycle close-out
choice (squash to one commit vs land each sub-step separately)
is made at close-out, not cycle start.

See also [`notes/substep-protocol.md`](notes/substep-protocol.md)
for the full protocol — when to use, per-substep contract
(`cargo test --bins` is non-negotiable), navigation, the validated
close-out squash recipe, and recovery. Revset primitives the
protocol relies on (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) live in
[`notes/jj-revsets.md`](notes/jj-revsets.md).

### Headings and entries that record a commit

A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
commit gets (see [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style)).
E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6) [[3]]`. For a multi-step cycle the `## Done` entry
uses the close-out commit's title.

This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
line a record up with its commit.

A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).

Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
example.

### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
other.

When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
normal ref direction).

**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
narrative; `Commits:` links them.

### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)

Commits: [[3]]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[notes/README.md](notes/README.md#reference-numbering)),
**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
40-hex SHA** in the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

### Pre-commit checklist

At cycle *start* (before the version bump): (a) backfill the
previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
commit's URL + full SHA — see
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references); (b) if
this cycle picks up a `## Todo` item (or re-scopes `## In
Progress` to absorb one), move that `## Todo` entry's text
into `## In Progress` and mirror it in the chores opener —
see [Versioning](#versioning).

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
   Versioning multi-step section.)
7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
   remove the picked-up item from `## In Progress` and add a
   one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) to `## Done`.
8. Update `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — add a section (header =
   provisional commit title; intro paragraph + any `###` design
   subsections; **no** per-file edit list — that's the commit
   body).
9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

## Sub-step Workflow

Larger work decomposes into named depths. The version-suffix
scheme nests:

- **Single step** — one change, one commit + push + finalize.

input.new_string:
### Versioning

Every plan starts with a version bump, and runs as one
**cycle** with three phases:

- **Preparation** — bump the version; populate `## In
  Progress` with the cycle's overview and plan (moving in a
  `## Todo` item if the cycle picks one up); open the chores
  section.
- **Work-N** — the implementation, in one or more commits.
- **Close-out** — move the `## In Progress` item to a
  `## Done` one-liner; finalize the chores section.

There is one protocol — this. "Single-step" / "multi-step"
are no longer separate protocols; a small change is just a
cycle with few Work commits.

**Pickup and close-out.** A plan that picks up a `## Todo`
item — or *re-scopes* an existing `## In Progress` ladder to
absorb one — **moves that entry into `## In Progress`** in
the Preparation commit:

- **Pickup:** the entry's text moves out of `## Todo` and
  into `## In Progress` — its overview and to-do list,
  followed by the plan ladder — so the section is
  self-describing, not a bare ladder. The chores opener
  **mirrors** that content so the cycle stays readable once
  `## In Progress` is cleared.
- **Close-out:** the item is removed from `## In Progress`
  and a one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) is added to
  `## Done`.

`## In Progress` is the sole live record of the work between
those points; the chores opener is the durable capture. A
cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves
all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
`## In Progress` work is a process bug.

**Numbering.** The version suffix carries the phase, with
one separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-`
once at the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level
after):

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs
subdividing into its own sub-cycle, append another level —
`X.Y.Z-3.0` (Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1`
/ `X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the
`.0` (`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation
later adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.

Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.

**Ordering caveat.** The one ordering guarantee semver gives
is that the bare `X.Y.Z` close-out outranks every `X.Y.Z-…`
dev version. Within the ladder it does *not* hold for nested
close-outs — `X.Y.Z-3` sorts *before* its children
`X.Y.Z-3.0` / `X.Y.Z-3.1`, because a prefix always precedes
its extensions. Cosmetic — nothing depends on within-ladder
order — but don't claim monotonicity.

**Why numeric suffixes** (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than
`-devN`: semver pre-release identifiers compare per spec and
Cargo accepts them. `-dev` adds no information the git log
doesn't carry and doubles typing per commit. The final
close-out commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) signals completion rather
than amending prior commits — history stays readable,
exploratory vs final commits obvious.

**push and squash are discretionary.** A cycle's commits
accumulate locally; **push** and **squash** are not modes
fixed at cycle start but actions taken when wanted:

- **push** — to back up work or to publish interim
  progress. Interim pushes are a judgement call; the
  **close-out push is mandatory** — a finished cycle must be
  published.
- **squash** — to tidy history, typically collapsing the
  cycle's commits into one before a publishing push. Whether
  to squash is decided at close-out, not cycle start.

The cycle *mechanics* — per-commit cargo cycle, the
commit-first review model, ochid trailers, `.claude`
cadence, the squash recipe, reviewing committed work — are
in [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol).

   marker.

Multi-step cycles place the plan ladder in
`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, below the picked-up item's
text, as a bullet list with `(done)` / `(current)` markers —
see the file's intro paragraph for the format. When starting a new step, the *first* edit is to mark
that step `(current)` in `notes/todo.md` — before any code/doc
work — so the In Progress view reflects what's actually
happening. The flip back to `(done)` is part of the pre-commit
checklist (item 6).

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
to see which commits were exploratory vs final.

For decomposition finer than `X.Y.Z-N` — sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M`)
or sub-sub-steps (`X.Y.Z-N.M-K`) — see
[Sub-step Workflow](#sub-step-workflow). The cycle close-out
choice (squash to one commit vs land each sub-step separately)
is made at close-out, not cycle start.

See also [`notes/substep-protocol.md`](notes/substep-protocol.md)
for the full protocol — when to use, per-substep contract
(`cargo test --bins` is non-negotiable), navigation, the validated
close-out squash recipe, and recovery. Revset primitives the
protocol relies on (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) live in
[`notes/jj-revsets.md`](notes/jj-revsets.md).
toolUseResult.newString:
### Versioning

Every plan starts with a version bump, and runs as one
**cycle** with three phases:

- **Preparation** — bump the version; populate `## In
  Progress` with the cycle's overview and plan (moving in a
  `## Todo` item if the cycle picks one up); open the chores
  section.
- **Work-N** — the implementation, in one or more commits.
- **Close-out** — move the `## In Progress` item to a
  `## Done` one-liner; finalize the chores section.

There is one protocol — this. "Single-step" / "multi-step"
are no longer separate protocols; a small change is just a
cycle with few Work commits.

**Pickup and close-out.** A plan that picks up a `## Todo`
item — or *re-scopes* an existing `## In Progress` ladder to
absorb one — **moves that entry into `## In Progress`** in
the Preparation commit:

- **Pickup:** the entry's text moves out of `## Todo` and
  into `## In Progress` — its overview and to-do list,
  followed by the plan ladder — so the section is
  self-describing, not a bare ladder. The chores opener
  **mirrors** that content so the cycle stays readable once
  `## In Progress` is cleared.
- **Close-out:** the item is removed from `## In Progress`
  and a one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) is added to
  `## Done`.

`## In Progress` is the sole live record of the work between
those points; the chores opener is the durable capture. A
cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves
all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
`## In Progress` work is a process bug.

**Numbering.** The version suffix carries the phase, with
one separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-`
once at the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level
after):

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs
subdividing into its own sub-cycle, append another level —
`X.Y.Z-3.0` (Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1`
/ `X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the
`.0` (`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation
later adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.

Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.

**Ordering caveat.** The one ordering guarantee semver gives
is that the bare `X.Y.Z` close-out outranks every `X.Y.Z-…`
dev version. Within the ladder it does *not* hold for nested
close-outs — `X.Y.Z-3` sorts *before* its children
`X.Y.Z-3.0` / `X.Y.Z-3.1`, because a prefix always precedes
its extensions. Cosmetic — nothing depends on within-ladder
order — but don't claim monotonicity.

**Why numeric suffixes** (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than
`-devN`: semver pre-release identifiers compare per spec and
Cargo accepts them. `-dev` adds no information the git log
doesn't carry and doubles typing per commit. The final
close-out commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) signals completion rather
than amending prior commits — history stays readable,
exploratory vs final commits obvious.

**push and squash are discretionary.** A cycle's commits
accumulate locally; **push** and **squash** are not modes
fixed at cycle start but actions taken when wanted:

- **push** — to back up work or to publish interim
  progress. Interim pushes are a judgement call; the
  **close-out push is mandatory** — a finished cycle must be
  published.
- **squash** — to tidy history, typically collapsing the
  cycle's commits into one before a publishing push. Whether
  to squash is decided at close-out, not cycle start.

The cycle *mechanics* — per-commit cargo cycle, the
commit-first review model, ochid trailers, `.claude`
cadence, the squash recipe, reviewing committed work — are
in [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol).
